1:
"O foolish Galatians, who hath bewitched you, that ye should not obey the truth, before whose eyes Jesus Christ hath been evidently set forth, crucified among you?" The witnesses note Paul's astonished rebuke of the Galatians' defection. Chrysostom notes that "bewitched" implies envy and a malignant eye, but here points to the deceivers' working on the Galatians by flattery, as the bewitched are harmed though they have done no wrong. The witnesses note Paul exclaiming at the foolish Galatians, asking who had bewitched them into disobeying the truth, when Christ crucified had been so plainly set before their eyes; the amazement at their seduction from the gospel — that Paul, marvelling that a people to whom Christ crucified had been preached so vividly (as if portrayed before their very eyes) could be charmed away from the truth by the Judaizers, treats their defection as a kind of bewitchment, so contrary was it to all they had seen and known; who hath bewitched you. The witnesses note O foolish Galatians, who hath bewitched you. The witnesses note Jesus Christ... evidently set forth, crucified.

The witnesses note Paul's astonished rebuke — who bewitched the Galatians from the truth, when Christ crucified had been so vividly set before their eyes?

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's astonished rebuke of their defection. The lesson the expositors draw is the amazement at their seduction from the gospel, so contrary to all they had seen of Christ crucified. The witnesses note the rebuke: "O foolish Galatians" — Paul rebukes them as foolish; the rebuke; the foolish; the astonished; the reproof; the marvel. The expositors observe the bewitching: "who hath bewitched you, that ye should not obey the truth" — Chrysostom notes the deceivers worked on them by flattery; the bewitching; the deceivers; the flattery; the not obey the truth; the charmed. The witnesses press the Christ set forth: "before whose eyes Jesus Christ hath been evidently set forth, crucified among you" — Christ crucified had been vividly set before their eyes; the Christ set forth; the crucified; the vividly; the before their eyes; the portrayed. The expositors draw the lesson on the amazement: Paul marvels that a people to whom Christ crucified was so plainly preached could be charmed away; the amazement; the marvels; the so plainly; the charmed away; the seduction. The witnesses observe the bewitchment: their defection was a kind of bewitchment — so contrary to all they had seen; the bewitchment; the defection; the contrary; the seen; the seduced. The expositors note the vivid preaching: Christ crucified had been preached so vividly, as if portrayed before their very eyes; the vivid preaching; the crucified; the portrayed; the eyes; the plain. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the O foolish Galatians who hath bewitched you, the Jesus Christ evidently set forth crucified, and the astonished rebuke. The verse thus records Paul's astonished rebuke of their defection — teaching the amazement at their seduction from the gospel, so contrary to all they had seen of Christ crucified.
2:
"This only would I learn of you, Received ye the Spirit by the works of the law, or by the hearing of faith?" The witnesses note Paul's decisive question about how they received the Spirit. Gill notes that, though there were many things he could put to them, he would ask this one question, which, if honestly answered, must expose their folly and end the controversy. Barnes notes the design is to convince them by appeal to their own experience. The witnesses note Paul asking the one decisive question — whether they received the Spirit by the works of the law or by the hearing of faith; the appeal to their own experience — that Paul rests the whole controversy on a single, unanswerable point: the Galatians had received the Spirit (with his gifts and graces) not when they kept the law, but when they heard and believed the gospel, so their own experience proved that the blessing came by faith, not by works; received ye the Spirit by works or by faith? The witnesses note received ye the Spirit by the works of the law. The witnesses note or by the hearing of faith?

The witnesses note Paul's decisive question — did they receive the Spirit by works of the law or by hearing of faith? — appealing to their own experience.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's decisive question about receiving the Spirit. The lesson the expositors draw is the appeal to their own experience, which proved the blessing came by faith, not works. The witnesses note the one question: "this only would I learn of you" — Gill notes Paul asks this one decisive question; the one question; the only; the decisive; the unanswerable; the appeal. The expositors observe the receiving: "received ye the Spirit by the works of the law" — did they receive the Spirit by the works of the law? the receiving; the Spirit; the works of the law; the question; the how. The witnesses press the hearing of faith: "or by the hearing of faith?" — or by the hearing of faith? the hearing of faith; the believing; the gospel; the faith; the alternative. The expositors draw the lesson on the appeal to experience: Paul appeals to their own experience — they received the Spirit when they believed, not when they kept the law; the appeal; the experience; the believed; the not the law; the proof. The witnesses observe the proof: their own experience proved the blessing came by faith; the proof; the experience; the blessing; the by faith; the demonstrated. The expositors note the decisive point: the question rests the whole controversy on this single, unanswerable point; the decisive point; the whole controversy; the single; the unanswerable; the rest. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the received ye the Spirit by the works of the law, the or by the hearing of faith, and the appeal to experience. The verse thus records Paul's decisive question about receiving the Spirit — teaching the appeal to their own experience, which proved the blessing came by faith, not works.
3:
"Are ye so foolish? having begun in the Spirit, are ye now made perfect by the flesh?" The witnesses note Paul's rebuke of the folly of turning from Spirit to flesh. Gill notes the wonder — "is it possible you should be so stupid? and do you, or can you continue so?" Barnes notes Paul hardly thought it credible they could pursue such a course after embracing the gospel so cordially. The witnesses note Paul asking, in astonishment, whether they are so foolish as to think that, having begun in the Spirit, they are now made perfect by the flesh; the absurdity of seeking perfection by the law after beginning by grace — that Paul exposes the backward folly of a people who started their Christian life by the Spirit (through faith) and now imagined they could be brought to completion by the flesh (the works of the law), as if the lower could perfect what the higher began; begun in the Spirit, perfected by the flesh? The witnesses note are ye so foolish? The witnesses note having begun in the Spirit, are ye now made perfect by the flesh?

The witnesses note Paul's rebuke — having begun in the Spirit, would they be perfected by the flesh? — the backward folly of seeking completion by the law.

So the witnesses make the verse the folly of turning from Spirit to flesh. The lesson the expositors draw is the absurdity of seeking perfection by the law after beginning by grace. The witnesses note the foolishness: "are ye so foolish?" — Gill notes the wonder at their stupidity; the foolishness; the so foolish; the stupid; the astonishment; the wonder. The expositors observe the begun in the Spirit: "having begun in the Spirit" — they began their Christian life in the Spirit (by faith, by grace); the begun; the in the Spirit; the by faith; the grace; the start. The witnesses press the perfected by flesh: "are ye now made perfect by the flesh?" — did they now think to be made perfect by the flesh (the works of the law)? the perfected; the by the flesh; the works of the law; the completion; the question. The expositors draw the lesson on the absurdity: it is absurd to seek perfection by the law after beginning by grace — the lower cannot perfect the higher; the absurdity; the perfection by the law; the after grace; the lower and higher; the backward. The witnesses observe the backward folly: the folly was backward — starting by the Spirit, ending by the flesh; the backward folly; the starting; the ending; the Spirit and flesh; the reversed. The expositors note the credibility: Barnes notes Paul hardly thought it credible they could pursue such a course; the credibility; the hardly; the such a course; the incredible; the marvel. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the are ye so foolish, the having begun in the Spirit are ye now made perfect by the flesh, and the folly. The verse thus records the folly of turning from Spirit to flesh — teaching the absurdity of seeking perfection by the law after beginning by grace.
4:
"Have ye suffered so many things in vain? if it be yet in vain." The witnesses note Paul's appeal to their past sufferings for the gospel. Gill notes the Galatians had suffered great reproach, afflictions, and persecutions for the gospel, which to those who persevere in the faith will not be in vain, but followed with eternal life and glory. The witnesses note Paul asking whether they had suffered so many things in vain — adding, "if it be yet in vain"; the warning that apostasy would waste their sufferings — that Paul presses on the Galatians the thought that, if they now abandoned the gospel for the law, all the persecutions they had endured for the faith would be thrown away and prove fruitless, yet tempering the warning with hope ("if it be yet in vain") that they might still recover and not lose all; suffered so many things in vain. The witnesses note have ye suffered so many things in vain? The witnesses note if it be yet in vain.

The witnesses note Paul's appeal to their past sufferings — would they suffer in vain if they abandoned the gospel? — tempered with hope of recovery.

So the witnesses make the verse the appeal to their past sufferings. The lesson the expositors draw is the warning that apostasy would waste their sufferings, tempered with hope of recovery. The witnesses note the sufferings: "have ye suffered so many things" — Gill notes the Galatians had suffered reproach, afflictions, persecutions for the gospel; the sufferings; the reproach; the afflictions; the persecutions; the endured. The expositors observe the in vain: "in vain?" — Paul asks whether they suffered in vain; the in vain; the wasted; the fruitless; the for nothing; the lost. The witnesses press the warning: if they abandoned the gospel for the law, their sufferings would be thrown away; the warning; the abandoned; the thrown away; the wasted; the apostasy. The expositors draw the lesson on the wasted sufferings: apostasy would make all their endured persecutions fruitless; the wasted sufferings; the apostasy; the fruitless; the persecutions; the lost. The witnesses observe the tempering hope: "if it be yet in vain" — Paul tempers the warning with hope that they might still recover; the tempering hope; the if it be yet; the recover; the hope; the not lost. The expositors note the persevering reward: to those who persevere, the sufferings will not be in vain, but followed by glory; the persevering; the reward; the not in vain; the glory; the perseverance. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the have ye suffered so many things in vain, the if it be yet in vain, and the appeal to their sufferings. The verse thus records the appeal to their past sufferings — teaching the warning that apostasy would waste their sufferings, tempered with hope of recovery.
5:
"He therefore that ministereth to you the Spirit, and worketh miracles among you, doeth he it by the works of the law, or by the hearing of faith?" The witnesses note the renewed question about the source of the Spirit and miracles. Gill notes the one who ministers the Spirit is God or Christ, who supplied the grace of the Spirit and the extraordinary gifts through the preaching of the gospel, not the law. The witnesses note Paul again asking whether the One who supplies them the Spirit and works miracles among them does so by the works of the law or by the hearing of faith; the divine bestowal tied to faith, not law — that Paul presses the same point a second time, now naming the ongoing supply of the Spirit and the working of miracles, and insisting that these divine operations came in connection with the believing reception of the gospel, never through law-keeping, so that God's own dealings vindicated justification by faith; ministereth the Spirit by works or by faith? The witnesses note he that ministereth to you the Spirit, and worketh miracles. The witnesses note by the works of the law, or by the hearing of faith?

The witnesses note the renewed question — does God supply the Spirit and work miracles by works of the law or hearing of faith? — God's dealings vindicating faith.

So the witnesses make the verse the renewed question about the Spirit and miracles. The lesson the expositors draw is that God's bestowal of the Spirit and miracles came by faith, not law. The witnesses note the minister: "he that ministereth to you the Spirit" — Gill notes the one ministering the Spirit is God or Christ; the minister; the ministereth the Spirit; the God; the Christ; the supplies. The expositors observe the miracles: "and worketh miracles among you" — and works miracles among them; the miracles; the worketh; the wonders; the operations; the gifts. The witnesses press the question: "doeth he it by the works of the law, or by the hearing of faith?" — does he do so by works of the law or hearing of faith? the question; the works of the law; the hearing of faith; the how; the source. The expositors draw the lesson on the divine bestowal: God's supply of the Spirit and miracles came in connection with the believing reception of the gospel, not law-keeping; the divine bestowal; the Spirit and miracles; the believing; the gospel; the not the law. The witnesses observe the second pressing: Paul presses the same point a second time — now the ongoing supply of the Spirit; the second pressing; the same point; the second time; the ongoing; the supply. The expositors note God's dealings vindicating faith: God's own dealings (giving the Spirit by faith) vindicated justification by faith; the God's dealings; the vindicating; the by faith; the justification; the proof. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he that ministereth to you the Spirit and worketh miracles, the by the works of the law or by the hearing of faith, and the divine bestowal by faith. The verse thus records the renewed question about the Spirit and miracles — teaching that God's bestowal came by faith, not law.
6:
"Even as Abraham believed God, and it was accounted to him for righteousness." The witnesses note the example of Abraham's justifying faith. Gill notes that, having shown the Spirit was received by faith not law, Paul passes by an easy transition to confirm justification by faith with the instance of Abraham, what Scripture says of him and the promise made to him. The witnesses note Paul citing that Abraham believed God, and it was accounted to him for righteousness; the great pattern of justification — that Paul grounds his doctrine in the case of Abraham (the father of the faithful), whose faith — not his works — was reckoned to him for righteousness (Genesis 15:6), so that the very father of the Jewish nation, in whom they gloried, was himself justified by faith, the abiding pattern for all his children; believed God, accounted for righteousness. The witnesses note even as Abraham believed God. The witnesses note it was accounted to him for righteousness.

The witnesses note the example of Abraham — he believed God and it was counted for righteousness — the great pattern of justification by faith.

So the witnesses make the verse the example of Abraham's justifying faith. The lesson the expositors draw is the great pattern of justification, Abraham justified by faith, not works. The witnesses note the believing: "even as Abraham believed God" — Abraham believed God; the believing; the Abraham; the believed; the faith; the trust. The expositors observe Gill's transition: Gill notes Paul confirms justification by faith with the instance of Abraham; the transition; the confirm; the justification by faith; the Abraham; the instance. The witnesses press the accounting: "it was accounted to him for righteousness" — his faith was accounted (reckoned, imputed) to him for righteousness (Genesis 15:6); the accounting; the reckoned; the imputed; the for righteousness; the faith. The expositors draw the lesson on the pattern: Abraham, the father of the faithful, was justified by faith — the abiding pattern; the pattern; the father of the faithful; the justified by faith; the abiding; the example. The witnesses observe the not works: Abraham's faith, not his works, was reckoned for righteousness; the not works; the faith; the reckoned; the righteousness; the imputed. The expositors note the father of the Jews: the very father of the Jewish nation, in whom they gloried, was justified by faith; the father; the Jewish nation; the gloried; the justified by faith; the pattern. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the even as Abraham believed God, the it was accounted to him for righteousness, and the pattern of justification. The verse thus records the example of Abraham's justifying faith — teaching the great pattern of justification, Abraham justified by faith, not works.
7:
"Know ye therefore that they which are of faith, the same are the children of Abraham." The witnesses note that believers are the true children of Abraham. Gill notes this is a plain point, not to be disputed — "ye know"; Barnes notes the inference that all who believe are the children of Abraham. The witnesses note Paul declaring that those who are of faith are the children of Abraham; the spiritual sonship of believers — that Paul draws the conclusion from Abraham's case: the true children of Abraham are not his natural descendants as such, but those who share his faith (the "of faith"), so that believing Gentiles, no less than believing Jews, are Abraham's real seed, his heirs by the bond of faith rather than blood; they which are of faith are the children of Abraham. The witnesses note they which are of faith. The witnesses note the same are the children of Abraham.

The witnesses note that believers — those "of faith" — are the true children of Abraham, his real seed by faith rather than blood.

So the witnesses make the verse the spiritual sonship of believers. The lesson the expositors draw is that the true children of Abraham are those who share his faith, not merely his natural descendants. The witnesses note the of faith: "they which are of faith" — Barnes notes those who believe; the of faith; the believers; the believing; the faith; the trust. The expositors observe the children: "the same are the children of Abraham" — they are the children of Abraham; the children; the of Abraham; the sons; the seed; the descendants. The witnesses press the spiritual sonship: the true children of Abraham are those who share his faith, not merely his natural descendants; the spiritual sonship; the share his faith; the not natural; the true children; the faith. The expositors draw the lesson on the believing Gentiles: believing Gentiles, no less than believing Jews, are Abraham's real seed; the believing Gentiles; the no less; the believing Jews; the real seed; the faith. The witnesses observe the bond of faith: believers are Abraham's heirs by the bond of faith rather than blood; the bond of faith; the heirs; the rather than blood; the faith; the spiritual. The expositors note the conclusion from Abraham: Paul draws the conclusion from Abraham's case — the of faith are his children; the conclusion; the Abraham's case; the of faith; the children; the inference. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the they which are of faith, the the same are the children of Abraham, and the spiritual sonship. The verse thus records the spiritual sonship of believers — teaching that the true children of Abraham are those who share his faith, not merely his natural descendants.
8:
"And the scripture, foreseeing that God would justify the heathen through faith, preached before the gospel unto Abraham, saying, In thee shall all nations be blessed." The witnesses note the gospel preached beforehand to Abraham. Gill notes the Scripture is here, by a figure, represented as foreseeing the event and speaking of it beforehand, designing God the author of Scripture, who foreknew that he would justify the Gentiles by faith. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the Scripture, foreseeing that God would justify the heathen through faith, preached the gospel beforehand to Abraham, saying that in him all nations would be blessed; the gospel announced in the promise — that the very promise to Abraham ("in thee shall all nations be blessed") was itself an early preaching of the gospel, foreshowing that God's way of justifying the Gentiles would be by faith, so that the doctrine the Judaizers opposed was no novelty but the ancient promise to the patriarch himself; preached before the gospel unto Abraham. The witnesses note God would justify the heathen through faith. The witnesses note in thee shall all nations be blessed.

The witnesses note the gospel preached beforehand to Abraham — "in thee shall all nations be blessed" — foreshowing God's justifying of the Gentiles by faith.

So the witnesses make the verse the gospel preached beforehand to Abraham. The lesson the expositors draw is that the promise to Abraham was an early preaching of the gospel, foreshowing justification of the Gentiles by faith. The witnesses note the foreseeing: "the scripture, foreseeing that God would justify the heathen through faith" — Gill notes the Scripture foresaw God would justify the Gentiles by faith; the foreseeing; the God would justify; the heathen; the through faith; the foreknew. The expositors observe the preaching: "preached before the gospel unto Abraham" — the Scripture preached the gospel beforehand to Abraham; the preaching; the before; the gospel; the to Abraham; the announced. The witnesses press the promise: "in thee shall all nations be blessed" — the promise that in Abraham all nations would be blessed; the promise; the all nations; the blessed; the in thee; the foreshowed. The expositors draw the lesson on the gospel in the promise: the promise to Abraham was itself an early preaching of the gospel; the gospel in the promise; the early preaching; the promise; the foreshowed; the gospel. The witnesses observe the justification of the Gentiles: the promise foreshowed God's way of justifying the Gentiles by faith; the justification; the Gentiles; the by faith; the foreshowed; the way. The expositors note the no novelty: the doctrine the Judaizers opposed was no novelty but the ancient promise; the no novelty; the Judaizers; the ancient promise; the patriarch; the old. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the God would justify the heathen through faith, the in thee shall all nations be blessed, and the gospel preached to Abraham. The verse thus records the gospel preached beforehand to Abraham — teaching that the promise to Abraham was an early preaching of the gospel, foreshowing justification of the Gentiles by faith.
9:
"So then they which be of faith are blessed with faithful Abraham." The witnesses note that believers share Abraham's blessing. Gill notes this is the apostle's conclusion from the instance of Abraham and the promise; it must be taken in a limited sense, not of every individual, but of those of the same faith with Abraham, believers in Christ who seek justification by faith. The witnesses note Paul concluding that those who are of faith are blessed together with faithful Abraham; the shared blessing of believers — that the conclusion of the argument is that all who believe (as Abraham believed) partake of the same blessing he received, namely justification and all its benefits, so that the believing Gentile is blessed in the company of the faithful patriarch, an heir of the promise made to him; blessed with faithful Abraham. The witnesses note they which be of faith. The witnesses note blessed with faithful Abraham.

The witnesses note that believers share Abraham's blessing — all who believe partake of the same justification he received, blessed with the faithful patriarch.

So the witnesses make the verse the shared blessing of believers. The lesson the expositors draw is that all who believe partake of the same blessing Abraham received. The witnesses note the of faith: "they which be of faith" — Gill notes those of the same faith with Abraham, believers in Christ; the of faith; the believers; the same faith; the in Christ; the believing. The expositors observe the blessed: "are blessed with faithful Abraham" — they are blessed together with faithful Abraham; the blessed; the with Abraham; the the faithful; the shared; the together. The witnesses press the shared blessing: all who believe partake of the same blessing Abraham received — justification and its benefits; the shared blessing; the same blessing; the justification; the benefits; the partake. The expositors draw the lesson on the believing Gentile: the believing Gentile is blessed in the company of the faithful patriarch; the believing Gentile; the company; the faithful patriarch; the blessed; the heir. The witnesses observe the heir of the promise: believers are heirs of the promise made to Abraham; the heir; the promise; the to Abraham; the inheritance; the blessed. The expositors note the limited sense: Gill notes this is of those of the same faith, not every individual; the limited sense; the same faith; the not every; the believers; the of faith. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the they which be of faith, the blessed with faithful Abraham, and the shared blessing. The verse thus records the shared blessing of believers — teaching that all who believe partake of the same blessing Abraham received.
10:
"For as many as are of the works of the law are under the curse: for it is written, Cursed is every one that continueth not in all things which are written in the book of the law to do them." The witnesses note that those seeking justification by the law are under its curse. Gill notes Paul says not "as many as were of the law" (the Jews), nor "as many as do the works of the law," but "as many as are of the works of the law" — those who seek justification and life by their own obedience. The witnesses note Paul declaring that as many as are of the works of the law are under the curse, since Scripture curses everyone who fails to continue in all things written in the law. The witnesses note the curse on the law-seeker — that those who rest their hope of acceptance on keeping the law place themselves under its curse, for the law demands perfect, perpetual obedience in all things, and pronounces a curse on the least failure, so that, no man keeping it perfectly, all who seek life by it are condemned by it; under the curse. The witnesses note as many as are of the works of the law are under the curse. The witnesses note cursed... that continueth not in all things.

The witnesses note that those who seek justification by the law are under its curse — for the law demands perfect obedience and curses the least failure.

So the witnesses make the verse the curse on the law-seeker. The lesson the expositors draw is that those who rest on law-keeping are under its curse, for the law demands perfect obedience and curses the least failure. The witnesses note the of the works: "as many as are of the works of the law" — Gill notes those who seek justification and life by their own obedience; the of the works; the seek justification; the own obedience; the law-seekers; the works. The expositors observe the curse: "are under the curse" — they are under the curse; the curse; the under; the condemned; the cursed; the doom. The witnesses press the demand: "cursed is every one that continueth not in all things which are written in the book of the law to do them" — the law curses everyone who fails to continue in all things; the demand; the all things; the continueth not; the perfect; the curse. The expositors draw the lesson on the perfect obedience required: the law demands perfect, perpetual obedience in all things; the perfect obedience; the perpetual; the all things; the demand; the required. The witnesses observe the curse on the least failure: the law pronounces a curse on the least failure; the least failure; the curse; the pronounced; the slightest; the condemned. The expositors note the condemnation of all: no man keeping it perfectly, all who seek life by it are condemned by it; the condemnation; the no man; the perfectly; the all; the condemned. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the as many as are of the works of the law are under the curse, the cursed that continueth not in all things, and the curse on the law-seeker. The verse thus records the curse on the law-seeker — teaching that those who rest on law-keeping are under its curse, for the law demands perfect obedience and curses the least failure.
11:
"But that no man is justified by the law in the sight of God, it is evident: for, The just shall live by faith." The witnesses note the proof that none is justified by the law. Gill notes that, though God's elect are justified in his eternal will, and believers manifestly in the court of conscience, no one is justified before God by the law. The witnesses note Paul declaring it evident that no man is justified by the law in God's sight, for the Scripture says, "the just shall live by faith"; the testimony of the prophet for justification by faith — that Paul clinches the argument with the word of Habakkuk, "the just shall live by faith," showing that the righteous man's life (his standing before God and his living from it) is by faith and not by law, so that justification before God has always been, by the testimony of the prophets themselves, the gift received through faith; the just shall live by faith. The witnesses note no man is justified by the law in the sight of God. The witnesses note the just shall live by faith.

The witnesses note the proof — none is justified by the law, for "the just shall live by faith" — the prophet's own testimony to justification by faith.

So the witnesses make the verse the proof that none is justified by the law. The lesson the expositors draw is the testimony of the prophet, "the just shall live by faith," for justification by faith. The witnesses note the evident: "that no man is justified by the law in the sight of God, it is evident" — Gill notes no one is justified before God by the law; the evident; the no man; the by the law; the in God's sight; the clear. The expositors observe the in God's sight: justification "in the sight of God" — before the divine tribunal; the in God's sight; the before God; the divine tribunal; the the court; the standing. The witnesses press the prophet's word: "for, The just shall live by faith" — Paul cites Habakkuk, "the just shall live by faith"; the prophet's word; the Habakkuk; the the just; the live by faith; the testimony. The expositors draw the lesson on living by faith: the righteous man's life (his standing before God and his living from it) is by faith, not law; the living by faith; the righteous; the standing; the from it; the faith. The witnesses observe the testimony to faith: the prophet's own testimony shows justification has always been by faith; the testimony; the prophet's; the always; the justification; the faith. The expositors note the clinching word: Paul clinches the argument with Habakkuk's word; the clinching; the argument; the Habakkuk; the word; the proof. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the no man is justified by the law in the sight of God, the the just shall live by faith, and the proof. The verse thus records the proof that none is justified by the law — teaching the testimony of the prophet, "the just shall live by faith," for justification by faith.
12:
"And the law is not of faith: but, The man that doeth them shall live in them." The witnesses note the opposed principles of law and faith. Gill notes the law is not of faith — it does not consist of faith in Christ, nor require it, nor direct a man to live by it on Christ's righteousness; it is the gospel that reveals Christ's righteousness and directs men to believe. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the law is not of faith, but says, "the man that doeth them shall live in them"; the contrasted ways of law and faith — that law and faith are opposite principles of acceptance: the law knows nothing of believing, but says simply "do and live," promising life only to perfect doing, while faith receives life as a gift; so the two cannot be mixed, and to seek justification by both is to confound contraries; the law is not of faith. The witnesses note the law is not of faith. The witnesses note the man that doeth them shall live in them.

The witnesses note the opposed principles — the law is not of faith but says "do and live," while faith receives life as a gift; the two cannot be mixed.

So the witnesses make the verse the opposed principles of law and faith. The lesson the expositors draw is that law and faith are opposite principles of acceptance, and cannot be mixed. The witnesses note the not of faith: "the law is not of faith" — Gill notes the law does not consist of or require faith in Christ; the not of faith; the not consist; the not require; the not believing; the contrast. The expositors observe the do and live: "the man that doeth them shall live in them" — the law says, do them and live in them; the do and live; the doeth; the live in them; the perfect doing; the principle. The witnesses press the opposed principles: law and faith are opposite principles of acceptance — doing versus believing; the opposed principles; the doing and believing; the opposite; the acceptance; the contrast. The expositors draw the lesson on the law's principle: the law knows nothing of believing, but says "do and live," promising life only to perfect doing; the law's principle; the do and live; the perfect doing; the no believing; the demand. The witnesses observe the faith's principle: faith receives life as a gift — not by doing; the faith's principle; the receives; the gift; the not doing; the believing. The expositors note the cannot be mixed: the two cannot be mixed — to seek justification by both is to confound contraries; the cannot be mixed; the both; the confound; the contraries; the opposite. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the law is not of faith, the the man that doeth them shall live in them, and the opposed principles. The verse thus records the opposed principles of law and faith — teaching that law and faith are opposite principles of acceptance, and cannot be mixed.
13:
"Christ hath redeemed us from the curse of the law, being made a curse for us: for it is written, Cursed is every one that hangeth on a tree:" The witnesses note the redemption from the curse by Christ made a curse. Gill notes the Redeemer is Christ, the Son of God, appointed and called to this work; as man a near kinsman to whom the right of redemption belonged, and as God able to accomplish it. The witnesses note Paul declaring that Christ has redeemed believers from the curse of the law by being made a curse for them, as it is written, "cursed is everyone that hangeth on a tree"; the substitutionary bearing of the curse — that Christ, to free his people from the law's curse under which all lay, took that curse upon himself, becoming a curse in their stead (his death on the tree being the very form of accursedness named in the law), so that by bearing the penalty he exhausted it, delivering those for whom he was made a curse; being made a curse for us. The witnesses note Christ hath redeemed us from the curse of the law. The witnesses note being made a curse for us... cursed... that hangeth on a tree.

The witnesses note Christ made a curse for us — redeeming his people from the law's curse by bearing it in their stead, the tree the form of accursedness named in the law.

14:
"That the blessing of Abraham might come on the Gentiles through Jesus Christ; that we might receive the promise of the Spirit through faith." The witnesses note the twofold purpose of Christ's redeeming work. Gill notes "the blessing of Abraham" is the same blessing Abraham enjoyed, justification by Christ's righteousness, and what was promised — that in his seed the Gentiles should be blessed; it chiefly regards justification, the deliverance from the law's curse. 15:
"Brethren, I speak after the manner of men; Though it be but a man's covenant, yet if it be confirmed, no man disannulleth, or addeth thereto." The witnesses note Paul's illustration from a confirmed human covenant. Gill notes that, whereas he had called them "foolish Galatians," Paul now softens his words and calls them "brethren," hoping well of them and imputing the fault rather to their teachers. Barnes notes Paul draws an illustration from common usage. The witnesses note Paul, addressing them tenderly as brethren, drawing an illustration from human practice — that even a man's covenant, once ratified, none annuls or adds to; the inviolability of a ratified covenant — that Paul argues from the lesser to the greater: if even a human agreement, once duly confirmed, is held sacred and cannot be set aside or altered, much more is God's confirmed covenant with Abraham unalterable, so that the law given long after could not annul it; no man disannulleth a confirmed covenant. 17:

The witnesses note that the later law cannot annul the earlier covenant. 18:
"For if the inheritance be of the law, it is no more of promise: but God gave it to Abraham by promise." The witnesses note that the inheritance is by promise, not law. Gill notes "the inheritance" means either eternal life, the gift of God through Christ, or particularly the blessing of justification promised in the covenant to Abraham and his spiritual seed, not obtained by obedience to the law. The witnesses note Paul arguing that if the inheritance is of the law, it is no longer of promise, but God gave it to Abraham by promise; the inheritance grounded in promise, not law — that Paul presses the incompatibility: the inheritance (justification and eternal life) is either by promise or by law, and cannot be both, and since God plainly bestowed it on Abraham as a free promise (before the law existed), it rests on grace and promise, not on law-keeping, which can add nothing to the gift; God gave it to Abraham by promise. The witnesses note if the inheritance be of the law, it is no more of promise. The witnesses note God gave it to Abraham by promise.

19:

The witnesses note the purpose and temporary office of the law. The witnesses note it was added because of transgressions. The witnesses note till the seed should come... ordained by angels in the hand of a mediator.

20:
"Now a mediator is not a mediator of one, but God is one." The witnesses note the much-debated saying about the mediator and the one God. Gill notes a mediator supposes two parties he stands between, at distance or disagreeing; Christ is the Mediator between God and men, and Moses was the mediator between God and Israel. The witnesses note Paul observing that a mediator implies more than one party, whereas God is one; the contrast of the law's mediated giving with the promise's directness — that Paul (in a notoriously compressed saying variously explained) draws a distinction between the law, which came through a mediator standing between two parties (God and the people, both bound by mutual conditions), and the promise, which God made of himself alone, as one party freely giving, so that the promise rests on God's single, unconditional grace, unlike the conditional, two-sided law; a mediator is not of one, but God is one. The witnesses note a mediator is not a mediator of one. The witnesses note but God is one.

The witnesses note the saying about the mediator — the law came through a mediator between two parties, but the promise God gave of himself alone; the directness of grace.

21:
"Is the law then against the promises of God? God forbid: for if there had been a law given which could have given life, verily righteousness should have been by the law." The witnesses note the denial that the law opposes the promise. Gill notes the objection — if the law was added for transgressions and the inheritance is by promise, then the law seems against the promises, as if God contradicted himself; Paul replies, God forbid. The witnesses note Paul denying that the law is against God's promises, since if any law could have given life, righteousness would indeed have been by the law; the law and promise not contrary, but the law unable to justify — that Paul repels the thought that law and promise are at odds (they serve different ends under one God), and grounds the impossibility of justification by law in a plain fact: no law was ever given that could impart life, for the law can command but not enliven, condemn but not justify, so righteousness never could come by it; if a law could give life. The witnesses note is the law then against the promises of God? God forbid. The witnesses note if there had been a law... which could have given life.

The witnesses note the law is not against the promise — but no law could give life; the law can command but not enliven, so righteousness never came by it.

So the witnesses make the verse the denial that law opposes promise. The lesson the expositors draw is that law and promise are not contrary, but the law cannot justify, for no law could give life. 22:
"But the scripture hath concluded all under sin, that the promise by faith of Jesus Christ might be given to them that believe." The witnesses note that Scripture shut all up under sin to make way for faith. Gill notes "the Scripture" means the law, or the whole Scripture, or God in it, who has shown and proved that all are under the power and dominion of sin, defiled by it and involved in its guilt. The witnesses note the scripture hath concluded all under sin. The witnesses note the promise by faith... given to them that believe.

23:
"But before faith came, we were kept under the law, shut up unto the faith which should afterwards be revealed." The witnesses note the church's confinement under the law before the gospel. 24:
"Wherefore the law was our schoolmaster to bring us unto Christ, that we might be justified by faith." The witnesses note the law as the schoolmaster leading to Christ. Gill notes the words should read "the law was our schoolmaster unto Christ" (the words "to bring us" being a supplement); the law performed this office until the coming of Christ, showing the church was in its minority, the Jews but children. The witnesses note the law was our schoolmaster. The witnesses note that we might be justified by faith.

The witnesses note the law as the schoolmaster (paidagogos) unto Christ — disciplining and, by exposing sin, conducting God's people to justification by faith.

The expositors observe the unto Christ: "to bring us unto Christ" — the schoolmaster conducted them unto Christ; the unto Christ; the conducted; the leading; the escorted; the to Christ. The witnesses press the discipline: the paidagogos disciplined and escorted a child under strict rule; the discipline; the paidagogos; the escorted; the strict rule; the guardian. The expositors draw the lesson on exposing sin: the law, by exposing sin and the impossibility of self-righteousness, conducts to Christ; the exposing sin; the impossibility; the self-righteousness; the conducts; the to Christ. The witnesses observe the justified by faith: "that we might be justified by faith" — that they might find justification by faith in Christ; the justified by faith; the in Christ; the the justification; the the law could not give; the faith. The expositors note the minority: Gill notes the church was in its minority, the Jews but children under the tutor; the minority; the children; the under the tutor; the the church; the immature. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the law was our schoolmaster, the that we might be justified by faith, and the schoolmaster unto Christ. The verse thus records the law as schoolmaster to Christ — teaching the disciplinary tutor that, by exposing sin, conducts God's people to justification by faith.
25:
"But after that faith is come, we are no longer under a schoolmaster." The witnesses note the believer's freedom from the schoolmaster once faith has come. Gill notes that since Christ, the object of faith, is come in the flesh, has fulfilled the law, and redeemed those under it from its bondage, curse, and condemnation. Barnes notes that after the scheme of salvation by faith is revealed, we are no longer under the pedagogue. The witnesses note Paul declaring that, after faith is come, believers are no longer under a schoolmaster; the passing of the law's tutelage with the gospel — that, now that the full revelation of faith has come in Christ (who fulfilled the law and freed his people), the disciplinary tutelage of the law has reached its end and is laid aside, the people of God grown to maturity in Christ and no longer kept in the childhood bondage of the schoolmaster; no longer under a schoolmaster. The witnesses note after that faith is come. The witnesses note we are no longer under a schoolmaster.

The witnesses note the believer no longer under the schoolmaster — once faith has come in Christ, the law's tutelage has reached its end, and God's people are grown to maturity.

So the witnesses make the verse the passing of the law's tutelage. The lesson the expositors draw is that, faith having come in Christ, the disciplinary tutelage of the law is laid aside. The witnesses note the faith come: "after that faith is come" — Gill notes Christ, the object of faith, is come; Barnes notes the scheme of salvation by faith revealed; the faith come; the Christ come; the revealed; the the gospel; the arrived. The expositors observe the no longer: "we are no longer under a schoolmaster" — believers are no longer under the schoolmaster (paidagogos); the no longer; the schoolmaster; the not under; the freed; the released. The witnesses press the passing tutelage: the disciplinary tutelage of the law has reached its end and is laid aside; the passing tutelage; the reached its end; the laid aside; the the law; the done. The expositors draw the lesson on Christ fulfilling: Christ, who fulfilled the law and freed his people, has ended the tutelage; the Christ fulfilling; the fulfilled; the freed; the ended; the tutelage. The witnesses observe the maturity: the people of God are grown to maturity in Christ — no longer in childhood bondage; the maturity; the grown; the in Christ; the not childhood; the bondage. The expositors note the freedom: believers are free from the schoolmaster's bondage; the freedom; the free; the schoolmaster; the bondage; the liberty. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the after that faith is come, the we are no longer under a schoolmaster, and the passing tutelage. The verse thus records the passing of the law's tutelage — teaching that, faith having come in Christ, the disciplinary tutelage of the law is laid aside.
26:
"For ye are all the children of God by faith in Christ Jesus." The witnesses note the believers' sonship by faith in Christ. Gill notes they are children of God not by nature (as Christ is the Son), nor by creation (as Adam and the angels), but by divine adoption, an act of God's rich and sovereign grace putting them among the children. The witnesses note Paul declaring that they are all the children of God by faith in Christ Jesus; the adoption of all believers as sons — that the privilege of being God's children belongs to all who believe, Gentile as well as Jew, received not by descent or law but by faith in Christ, so that the whole company of believers, joined to the Son, share by grace the standing of sons of God; children of God by faith. The witnesses note ye are all the children of God. The witnesses note by faith in Christ Jesus.

The witnesses note the believers' sonship — all are children of God by faith in Christ, received not by descent or law but by adoption through grace.

So the witnesses make the verse the sonship of believers by faith. The lesson the expositors draw is the adoption of all believers as sons of God, received by faith in Christ, not by descent or law. The witnesses note the children of God: "ye are all the children of God" — Gill notes they are children by divine adoption, an act of grace; the children of God; the adoption; the grace; the sons; the put among. The expositors observe the all: "ye are all" — all believers, Gentile as well as Jew; the all; the believers; the Gentile and Jew; the whole; the company. The witnesses press the by faith: "by faith in Christ Jesus" — by faith in Christ Jesus; the by faith; the in Christ; the not descent; the not law; the faith. The expositors draw the lesson on the adoption: the privilege of sonship is by adoption, God's sovereign grace; the adoption; the privilege; the sovereign grace; the put among the children; the sons. The witnesses observe the not by descent: the sonship is received not by descent or law but by faith; the not by descent; the not law; the by faith; the received; the grace. The expositors note the joined to the Son: believers, joined to the Son, share by grace the standing of sons; the joined to the Son; the share; the standing; the sons; the grace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the ye are all the children of God, the by faith in Christ Jesus, and the sonship by faith. The verse thus records the sonship of believers by faith — teaching the adoption of all believers as sons of God, received by faith in Christ, not by descent or law.
27:
"For as many of you as have been baptized into Christ have put on Christ." The witnesses note the union with Christ signified in baptism. Gill notes this supposes some baptized in water may not be baptized into Christ; but those truly and rightly baptized into Christ have put him on. The witnesses note Paul declaring that as many as have been baptized into Christ have put on Christ; the putting on of Christ — that those who are truly baptized into Christ (united to him, the inward reality the outward sign represents) have "put on" Christ as a garment, clothed with him, with his righteousness for their justification and his image for their sanctification, the believer so joined to Christ that he is reckoned and renewed in him; have put on Christ. The witnesses note as many of you as have been baptized into Christ. The witnesses note have put on Christ.

The witnesses note those baptized into Christ have put on Christ — clothed with him, with his righteousness for justification and his image for sanctification.

So the witnesses make the verse the union with Christ in baptism. The lesson the expositors draw is the putting on of Christ, the believer clothed with him for justification and sanctification. The witnesses note the baptized into Christ: "as many of you as have been baptized into Christ" — Gill notes those truly baptized into Christ (united to him); the baptized into Christ; the united; the truly; the into him; the the reality. The expositors observe the not all water-baptized: Gill notes some baptized in water may not be baptized into Christ; the not all; the water-baptized; the into Christ; the inward; the reality. The witnesses press the put on Christ: "have put on Christ" — they have put on Christ (as a garment, clothed); the put on Christ; the garment; the clothed; the union; the wear. The expositors draw the lesson on the clothing: the believer is clothed with Christ — his righteousness for justification, his image for sanctification; the clothing; the Christ's righteousness; the justification; the his image; the sanctification. The witnesses observe the union: the believer is so joined to Christ that he is reckoned and renewed in him; the union; the joined; the reckoned; the renewed; the in him. The expositors note the inward reality: the putting on is the inward reality the outward sign of baptism represents; the inward reality; the the sign; the represents; the baptism; the union. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the as many as have been baptized into Christ, the have put on Christ, and the union with Christ. The verse thus records the union with Christ in baptism — teaching the putting on of Christ, the believer clothed with him for justification and sanctification.
28:
"There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither bond nor free, there is neither male nor female: for ye are all one in Christ Jesus." The witnesses note the abolition of distinctions in Christ. Gill notes there were such persons in being and in the churches, but the meaning is that there is no difference between them: in the business of justification and salvation it signified nothing whether one was a Jew or a Greek. The witnesses note Paul declaring that in Christ there is neither Jew nor Greek, bond nor free, male nor female, for all are one in Christ Jesus; the oneness of all believers in Christ — that the distinctions of nation, social rank, and sex, however real in their place, are abolished as grounds of standing before God, for in the matter of justification, sonship, and the inheritance every believer stands on the same footing, all made one in Christ, the middle wall broken down; ye are all one in Christ Jesus. The witnesses note neither Jew nor Greek... bond nor free... male nor female. The witnesses note ye are all one in Christ Jesus.

The witnesses note the abolition of distinctions — neither Jew nor Greek, bond nor free, male nor female — all one in Christ, on the same footing before God.

So the witnesses make the verse the oneness of all believers in Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that the distinctions of nation, rank, and sex are abolished as grounds of standing before God. The witnesses note the no distinction: "there is neither Jew nor Greek" — Gill notes there is no difference between them in justification; the no distinction; the neither Jew nor Greek; the no difference; the justification; the standing. The expositors observe the rank and sex: "neither bond nor free... neither male nor female" — neither bond nor free, male nor female; the rank; the bond and free; the male and female; the social; the sex. The witnesses press the all one: "ye are all one in Christ Jesus" — all are one in Christ Jesus; the all one; the in Christ; the united; the same; the oneness. The expositors draw the lesson on the abolished distinctions: the distinctions, real in their place, are abolished as grounds of standing before God; the abolished; the distinctions; the in their place; the grounds; the standing. The witnesses observe the same footing: in justification, sonship, and inheritance every believer stands on the same footing; the same footing; the justification; the sonship; the inheritance; the equal. The expositors note the middle wall broken: the middle wall of partition is broken down, all made one; the middle wall; the broken down; the all one; the united; the partition. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the neither Jew nor Greek bond nor free male nor female, the ye are all one in Christ Jesus, and the oneness in Christ. The verse thus records the oneness of all believers in Christ — teaching that the distinctions of nation, rank, and sex are abolished as grounds of standing before God.
29:
"And if ye be Christ's, then are ye Abraham's seed, and heirs according to the promise." The witnesses note the believers' heirship as Abraham's seed in Christ. Gill notes they are Christ's, not only by profession but by possession — Christ dwelling in their hearts by faith, having his Spirit of regeneration and adoption. The witnesses note Paul concluding that if they are Christ's, then they are Abraham's seed and heirs according to the promise; the heirship of all who belong to Christ — that the whole argument closes with this: those who are Christ's (the one true Seed, v.16) are, in him, themselves Abraham's seed and so heirs of the promise, the believing Gentile thereby brought into the full inheritance promised to Abraham, not by the law but by union with Christ and the bond of the promise; heirs according to the promise. The witnesses note if ye be Christ's, then are ye Abraham's seed. The witnesses note and heirs according to the promise.

The witnesses note the believers' heirship — if they are Christ's, they are Abraham's seed and heirs of the promise, in the one true Seed, by union with Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the heirship of all who belong to Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that those who are Christ's are, in him, Abraham's seed and heirs of the promise. The witnesses note the Christ's: "if ye be Christ's" — Gill notes they are Christ's by possession, Christ dwelling in them; the Christ's; the by possession; the dwelling; the his Spirit; the belong. The expositors observe the Abraham's seed: "then are ye Abraham's seed" — then they are Abraham's seed; the Abraham's seed; the in him; the the seed; the descendants; the spiritual. The witnesses press the heirs: "and heirs according to the promise" — and heirs according to the promise; the heirs; the the promise; the inheritance; the according; the the gift. The expositors draw the lesson on the heirship in Christ: those who are Christ's (the one true Seed, v.16) are, in him, Abraham's seed and heirs; the heirship; the in Christ; the the one Seed; the Abraham's seed; the heirs. The witnesses observe the Gentile brought in: the believing Gentile is thereby brought into the full inheritance promised to Abraham; the Gentile brought in; the full inheritance; the promised; the Abraham; the included. The expositors note the by union not law: the inheritance comes not by the law but by union with Christ and the bond of the promise; the by union; the not the law; the union with Christ; the bond; the promise. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the if ye be Christ's then are ye Abraham's seed, the and heirs according to the promise, and the heirship in Christ. The verse thus records the heirship of all who belong to Christ — teaching that those who are Christ's are, in him, Abraham's seed and heirs of the promise.
